title: Command-Line History
subtitle: ↑/↓ for prefix-match, q: for the full window.
summary: From the : prompt, ↑ and ↓ scroll history filtered by what you've already typed. q: opens a full editable history window — every Ex command you've ever run, in a buffer you can edit and re-execute.

blocks.text: Vim remembers everything you type at the {key::} prompt. Two interfaces let you re-use it: arrow scrolling and the full *history window*.

rows: Scroll history filtered by current prefix

blocks.text: If you've typed `:s` then press {key:↑}, only previous commands starting with `s` are shown. {key:Ctrl-P} ignores the prefix and walks plain history.

sequence.note: Opens a buffer with full Ex command history
Move up to a previous command
Or close the window without running anything

blocks.title: Why q: Is Powerful
blocks.text:
{key:q}{key::} opens a *real Vim buffer* containing your command history. You can:

  • Edit any line (fix a typo) before pressing {key:Enter} to run it.
  • Yank/delete history lines.
  • Search history with {key:/}.
  • Use Visual mode to select a region and execute it as multiple commands (with `:@"` after yanking).

{key:q}{key:/} and {key:q}{key:?} do the same for **search history** — every {key:/} or {key:?} pattern you've used.

**Common gotcha:** people accidentally hit {key:q}{key::} when intending {key::}{key:q} (quit). If a buffer pops up with old commands, you didn't break Vim — you opened the history window. Press {key::}{key:q} to close it.
From the {key::} prompt, {key:Ctrl-F} also opens this window — so {key:q}{key::} is just a Normal-mode shortcut for the same thing.
